transcript.text: I just got back from that conference, and every time I think about the presentation, I feel like a failure. The upcoming review feels like a test of my worth, and I'm terrified of being perceived as incompetent. I keep setting impossibly high standards for myself, which just amplifies the anxiety as we face the restructuring of the team.

The conference presentation still feels really raw for you, and it's like this weight is carrying over into your thoughts about the upcoming review. You're not just worried about the review itself, but also about being seen as competent, which is a lot of pressure to put on yourself. It sounds like these impossibly high standards you set for yourself are making everything feel even more overwhelming, especially with the team restructuring looming. Yeah, that's a really challenging spot to be in.
I know you're right, but I keep thinking I let everyone down. My standards are impossible, and the team changes just add to the pressure.

The pressure you're putting on yourself is immense, and it's like you're shouldering the entire team's expectations. You're not just worried about the review, but also about being seen as competent, and that's a heavy burden to carry. It's understandable that you'd feel like you let everyone down, given the high standards you set for yourself. Yeah, it's a really tough spot to be in, especially with the team changes looming.

I keep replaying that presentation and feel like a failure, and my standards are so high that I can't even see a path forward. The restructuring only makes it worse, and I'm not sure how to prove my worth to them.

You're still carrying the weight of that presentation with you, and it's like it's casting a shadow over your thoughts about the upcoming review. The pressure you're putting on yourself to meet these incredibly high standards is overwhelming, and the team restructuring is only adding to that feeling of being lost. It's like you're trying to prove your worth, but the path forward feels unclear. Yeah, that's a really challenging spot to be in.
I keep mapping every misstep to a failure metric; the restructuring feels like a variable I can't control. I'm not sure how to quantify my worth in the review.

You're carrying the weight of those high standards you set for yourself, and it's like every misstep is being measured against this impossible metric. The restructuring feels like an unpredictable factor that's adding to your anxiety. Can you think of any ways you've demonstrated your worth in the past that don't rely on these metrics?

Honestly, I can't see any concrete successes right now – the last campaign's ROI was up 12% but that feels like a small win compared to the failure at the conference. I'm still stuck in a loop of 'what if' scenarios that make me feel incompetent.

You're having trouble seeing your successes right now, and that 12% ROI increase from the last campaign feels overshadowed by the conference presentation. It's like that one event is coloring your whole perspective on your abilities. I want to acknowledge that it takes a lot of courage to admit when we're feeling stuck in a loop of self-doubt. Can you tell me more about what's making it hard for you to recognize your accomplishments, like that ROI increase, as meaningful successes?

Honestly, I keep measuring that 12% as a negligible win because my internal benchmark is set by the conference fiasco. I keep thinking that if I had delivered a flawless presentation, I'd be seen as competent, so anything less feels like a failure. It's like I'm stuck in a self‑fulfilling loop where my own standards keep discounting my successes.

You're measuring your successes against this impossible standard set by the conference, and it's making even a 12% ROI increase feel insignificant. It's like your internal benchmark is overshadowing everything else, including your accomplishments. That must be really frustrating, feeling like nothing is good enough because it's not flawless. Can you think of any times when you've achieved something and felt truly proud of yourself, regardless of how it measured up to your standards?

Honestly, I can't think of a moment that feels genuinely proud; every success I see is measured against that conference disaster, so the 12% ROI feels negligible. This constant self‑critique makes me doubt my worth, especially with the team's restructuring looming.

The weight of your self-critique is really holding you back from seeing your accomplishments as meaningful, and it's understandable that the conference presentation still feels like a significant setback. It's like every success is being measured against that one event, making it hard for you to feel genuinely proud of yourself. Yeah, it's tough to break out of that cycle of self-doubt, especially with the team's restructuring looming. I want to acknowledge how brave it is to admit when we're struggling with these feelings, and I'm here to support you in exploring this further.

Thanks for the support, but I still feel like I'm not enough and the restructuring will just expose my flaws. Every time I think about the upcoming changes, I feel like a failure, and my standards keep pushing me further down.

The weight of your self-critique is really holding you back, and it's understandable that the team's restructuring is making you feel like your flaws will be exposed. It's like every change is a potential reminder that you're not meeting your own incredibly high standards. Yeah, that's a really tough spot to be in, and it takes a lot of courage to admit when we're feeling this way. I want to acknowledge how hard it must be to feel like you're not enough, especially when it seems like everything is changing around you.

I feel like I'm trapped in a self‑fulfilling loop; the restructuring is just another variable I can't control, and the only metric that matters is that conference disaster. I keep measuring every win against that fail, so the 12% ROI feels negligible. If I can't find a way to quantify my worth beyond that single mistake, I'm stuck.

You're feeling trapped in this cycle of self-doubt, and it's like every success is being overshadowed by the memory of that conference presentation. The restructuring is just adding to your anxiety, making you feel like your flaws will be exposed. I want to acknowledge how hard it must be to feel like you're not enough, especially when it seems like everything is changing around you. It takes a lot of courage to admit when we're feeling this way, and I'm here to support you in exploring this further. Yeah, it's tough to break out of that cycle of self-doubt.

I keep trying to reframe the 12% ROI as a win, but the conference still feels like the yardstick. It feels like every metric is a reminder that I'm underperforming. I don't know if I can let go of that standard before the review.

It's really tough to let go of a standard that's been set, especially when it feels like every metric is a reminder that you're underperforming. The conference still feels like a significant setback, and it's understandable that you're measuring every win against that. Yeah, it's like that one event is coloring your whole perspective on your abilities. I want to acknowledge how hard it must be to feel like you're not meeting your own expectations, especially with the review looming. Can you tell me more about what you think would need to happen for you to feel like you're letting go of that standard and seeing your successes as meaningful?
